translation_text: [recto] . . .; to the register . . . -esimos or of . . .; of the other 50 liters of gold according . . .; in which . . . do not set out records . . .; [l. 5] of the posted summons . . .; in the law court of the praeses . . .; of the all prevailing God and our Lord . . .; Flavius Anastasios son of immortal Augusts Emperor . . .; in the stated the highest . . .; following . . .

title: Petition
summary: Petition.  Someone from the plaintiff's village had married a woman from another village and had become a leader in raids against his former villages.  The petition seems to be an appeal to the headman of the village to restrain his people from their lawless acts.  If no such action is taken, the plaintiffs will act for themselves, apparently without recourse to law or higher authority
provenance: Provenance Unknown
translation_text: From . . . I have received the letters from your brotherhood . . . serious matters, concerning which those who had been telling such things to you do not owe you . . . before three years he departed from our village and went down to . . . [l. 5] and he took a wife there and has done a lot of harm to our possessions . . . of the shepherds, and we will be indebted to you to investigate the wrongdoings by those people of your village unless these . . . we are forced to commit acts not agreeable to God and the people . . . of the matters, and may God, our Lord, persuade you . . . [Verso] Given to master Sarapion . . . of the polis . . . from Theodosios.

title: Petition [I? AD]
summary: Petition which concerns private land, with subscription at the bottom. Dated 35th and 36th year of an unknown Emperor: script does not look like Augustan era
provenance: Garrett Deposit 1924, no. IX 54 in H. I. Bell's inventory

title: Petition [II/III AD]
summary: Petition: content unclear
provenance: Given to Princeton University in 1957 by Edmund H. Kase in memory of Professor Allan Chester Johnson

title: Magical Text
summary: Magical text consisting of an acrostic, some magical symbols and an erotic incantation
translation_text: [11 lines of magical words and symbols] Drive Ptolemais whom Helene bore to Ptolemaios whom [l. 15] Didyme bore. Inflame the liver and the spirit and the heart and the soul of this woman until she rushes forth and comes, Ptolemais, whom [l. 20] Helene bore to Ptolemaios whom Didyme bore, now, fast.

title: List [ca. 140 AD]
summary: Petition to the prefect Gaius Avidius Heliodorus (prefect 140-143 AD), from Marcus Valerius Gemellus, physician, seeking release from acting as supervisor of certain estates near thevillages of Bacchias and Hephaestias, confiscated by the government.  He makes the claim on the grounds of ill health and the fact that he has supervised these estates for four years                                                                     He a lso seeks exemption from other liturgies because of his profession
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1901

title: Petition to the Prefect
summary: Petition to the prefect.  Content is unclear, but in some way, the patria potestes of someone (the petitioner?) is in some way connected with the problem.  Refers to a woman named Aurelia Isarion
provenance: From Oxyrhynchus
translation_text: . . . to the most eminent prefect of Egypt from; . . . residing in the illustrious and most illustrious city of Oxyrhynchos; . . . to the husband of the union (marriage); . . . authority of the husband in respect of; [l. 5] . . . nothing is hidden; . . . suitably and he himself loving; . . . Aurelia Isarion to whom all; . . . and by having me father children by her; . . . for that reason . . . her from our; [l. 10] . . . and before that buy a house; in the quarter of Dromos Gymnasios from Aurelia Besous; . . . she herself be assigned to me accordingly; . . . documents but to the; . . . the land under the guardianship so that from; [l. 15] . . . I present the accounts of good repute to; . . . the subscription through the most beneficent; . . . I will agree. Be well; . . . increase.

title: Petition [123 AD]
summary: Petition to Hermodoros, basilikogrammateus and acting strategos, from Didymos, a minor, complaining of fraud on the part of his mother, Matrina, in her capacity as his guardian.  It is alleged that Matrina, after various acts of bad faith, had obtained possession of a deed belonging to Didymos and demanded in exchange for it a document absolving her from all claims in                                                                     connection with the guardianship
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society

title: Petition to a Strategus(?)
summary: Opening lines of a petition addressed to  Tiberius Claudius Sara[pion], who was, perhaps, a strategos, from Euporos Apete[ .  Refers to a daughter of Euporos
provenance: From Oxyrhynchus
translation_text: To Tiberius Claudius Sarapion, strategos(?) from Euporos son of Apete . . . from Oxyrhynchus . . . of my daughter . . .

title: Petition to Strategus
summary: Petition to the strategos of the Herakleides division of the Arsinoite nome in which the writer of the petition reports an accident involving severe injury to his brother.  Although the details are uncertain, it appears that one of the brothers, Atammon, fell from a roof in the village of Kaminou, the accident having been in some way occasioned by an attack by Libyans. The injured man has received no medical attention and his brother petitions the strategos to take some action so that Atammon's life may be spared
provenance: From Philadelphia
translation_text: To Aurelius Apollonius, also called Hierax, strategos of the Herakleides division of the Arsinoite nome from Aurelius Asoeis, son of Pauseris from the village of [l. 5] Philadelphia concerning my brother Atammon residing in the village of Kaminou. Since during the raid on us of the Lybians my brother [l. 10] Atammon yesterday fell from the roof of the house, which my brother owns in the same village. He is in critical condition and has numerous fractures, [l 15] and therefore since he is in critical condition, I am supplying this petition asking to have it registered in case the human thing happens to him [i.e. in case my brother dies]. Be well. [l. 20] Asoeis, 40 years old, with a scar on the left shin. The 6th year of the Emperors Caesars Publius Licinnius Valerianus and Publius Licinnius Valerianus Gallienus, Germanici Maximi [l. 25] Pii Felices, and Publius Licinnius Cornelius Saloninus Valerianus the most illustrious Caesar, Augusti, the .1st of Thoth.

title: Petition to Strategus
summary: Opening lines of a petition from Aurelia Asklatarion, also called Coprilla, to Aurelius Spartiates, also called Chairemon, strategos of the Hermopolite nome
provenance: From Hermopolis
translation_text: To Aurelius Spartiates, also called Chairemon, strategos of the Hermolite nome, from Aurelia Asklaterion, also called Koprilla, daughter of Herminos, also called Asklepiades, son of Chairemon from Hermopolis, who is registered [l. 5] in the quarter of Frourios, to the west, with Aurelius Agathos son of Daimon also called Hermaios acting with her . . . attendant of the council of the city of Hermopolis . . .

title: Property Return [131 AD]
summary: Return of house property, submitted by Sambous, daughter of Pnepheros, son of Dionysios to the keepers of the property registers of the Arsinoite nome, the ex-gymnasiarchs Dios, also called Apollonios, and Herodes, also called Diogenes; Sambous' brother, Allothon(?) is acting for her in registering a half share in a house and courtyard
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1901

title: Petition [131 AD]
summary: Return of property for a half share in a house and courtyard submitted by Alexion to the keepers of the property registers of the Arsinoite nome, Dios and Herodes (see apis.p0616); the signature of Dios appears at the top in a different hand; dated in Mesore of the fifteenth year of Hadrian
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1901

title: Receipt [276-282 AD]
summary: Receipt for payments made to, or in connection with soldiers in Egypt under the command of the praepositus, Rufinus;  this praepositus is not the Rufinus of P.Dura 54, not the Rufinus of Tab. Vindol. 3B, 38; the nomen Aurelius is used in the Latin text , but not in the Greek; dated in the reign of Probus

title: Receipt (for Garden Tax)
summary: Receipt for payment of garden taxes for the year 145 AD.  Paid by Athenarion the elder, daughter of Herodes
provenance: From Theadelphia?
translation_text: The 9th year of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrian Antoninus Augustus Pius, the 7th of Tybi. The elder Athenarion, daughter of Herodos, has paid through Zosimos and his associates in collecting taxes in silver the garden-taxes for the 8th year at [l. 5] Theadelphia, for vineyard 2440 drachmas in bronze, for the naubion beyond tax 120 drachmas, for additional fees 515 drachmas, for the land tax 1625 drachmas, additional fees 125 drachmas, for exchange 80, charge for preparing the receipt of drachmas in silver the gratuity of Dionyios 8 drachmas in silver, totals 8 drachmas additional fees 3 1/2 obols and 8 into the account for Phamenoth. . . . of the 8th year, for the vineyard 1440 drachmas, for the garden 570 drachmas, for the naubion 105 drachmas, additional fees 340 drachmas, for the land-tax 1400 drachmas, additional fees 110 drachmas, for the exchange 65 drachmas, for preparing the receipt [l. 10] of 8 drachmas in silver 2 drachmas, totals 2 drachmas and additional fees.

title: Amulet
summary: Fever amulet, with a triangular charm followed by a prayer(?) for recovery from fever
provenance: Unknown
translation_text: [9 lines of botryoeidos charm] Good angels, the Lords, allay the fever of –dias whom Sophia bore, this very day, this very hour and this very moment at once, at once.

title: Receipt for telos katalochismon
summary: Receipt for 118 dr. paid for taxes on 26 1/2 arouras of katoikic land in the neighborhood of Pela in the allotment of Diodoros, son of Demokrates.  Paid to Neilos and Sarapammon tax collectors, by Origeneia, daughter of Marcus [  ] Niger (?) and Demetria daughter of Sarapodoros.  Origeneia is a citizen of Alexandria, as is her representative in the transaction Apollonios alias Didymos, son of Apollonios (?), grandson of Dioskoros.  The land was ceded to Origeneia and Demetria by Tbekis, daughter of Pausiris and Kollauchis, from Pela
provenance: From the Oxyrhynchite nome
translation_text: Neilos and Sarapammon, farmers of the tax on catoecic registry for the Oxyrhynchite and other nomes, through their agent, Nepotianos also called Apollonios, [l. 4] to Orgineia, citizen, daughter of Marcus . . . Niger and Demetria, daughter of Sarapodoros, through Apollonios also called Didymos, son of Apollonios, grandson of Dioskoros, of the Sosicosmian tribe and Althean deme, greetings. [l. 8] You have paid to us as taxes on the 26 1/2 catoecic arouras in the neighborhood of Pela in the allotment of Diodoros son of Demokrates, which you have received in cession through the [l. 12] record-bureau in Pachon of the past 3rd yr. from Tbekis, daughter of Pausiris and Kollauchis, from Pela, the one hundred eighteen drachmas owed, including a gratuity in money, total 118 dr. Year 4 of the Emperor Caesar Lucius [l. 16] Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, Phamenoth 20.

title: Receipt [II/III AD]
summary: Customs house receipt for payment of the dues on three donkey loads (9 artabas) of orobos being exported through the customs house at Bacchias
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1901

title: Custom-House Receipt
summary: Customs house receipt for payment of the limen Mempheos on a small shipment of dates being exported through the customs house at Philadelphia
provenance: From Philadelphia
translation_text: Paid through the gate-house of Philadelphia on the account of "Memphis-harbor tax" (a customs duty paid on crossing the border between Upper and Lower Egypt at Memphis) by Aurelius Tesenouphis exporting on one donkey, 1, one artaba of dates, 1. [l. 5] The 4th (year?), the twenty third of Epeiph, 23rd . . .

title: Receipt [232/231 BC]
summary: Receipt for 11 1/6 artabas of wheat, probably for phylakitikon and iatrikon. Dated Year 16 (of Euergetes) Phaophi 11
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1907

title: Pilot's Receipt
summary: Shipping receipt in which the kybernetes in command of three boats  engaged in the transportation of grain to Alexandria, acknowledges to the sitologoi of Arsinoite Philadelphia the loading of 8627 1/2 artabas of wheat
provenance: From Philadelphia
translation_text: To the sitologoi of Philadelphia, in the Heracleides division of the Arsinoite nome from Achais, son of Phathres, the son of Petephnouthis, from around the village of Athra [l. 5] of the Prosopeite nome, with the guarantee of Didymos, who is also called Hermeios, and with the assistance of the clerk Serenus, son of Didymos, son of Althaieus. I, a pilot of three ships with the following cargo: 8700 artabas, with one entry for the one percent tax, 87 1/2 artabas and with the one and a half percent tax being 43 1/2, [l. 10] totals 8830 1/2 artabas. According to which he has willingly unloaded 200 artabas, with the one percent tax being 2 artabas, and the one and a half percent, 1 artaba, which totals 203 artabas of wheat. What remains for the embole is 8500 artabas, with the one percent tax being 85 artabas, and the one and a half percent 42 1/2, which totals 8627 1/2 [8827 1/2, pap.]. [l. 15] At the command of Domittius Rufus, procurator of Neapolis, I agree to have had measured and loaded (the cargo) by you at the harbor of Kerke, so that it can be conveyed to Alexandria for the administration of Neapolis, [l. 20] at the instruction of Ptolemaios, royal scribe of the Themistes division, who is serving in the place of both the strategos and [l. 25] the royal scribe of the Heracleides division.

title: Receipt [ca. 155 AD]
summary: For one artaba of wheat paid out from the deposit of Diogas, by the silologoi for the topos of Monimou; contains the signature of the recipient
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1907

title: Receipt [179-180 AD]
summary: Receipt for twenty-eight artabas of wheat paid to the public officials
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1907

title: Receipt [179-180 AD]
summary: Receipt for payment of sixty-five artabas of wheat paid out by the sitologoi of the eastern toparchy
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1907

title: Receipt [IV/V AD]
summary: Receipt for one artaba of grain
provenance: Garrett Deposit 1924, no. II 218 in H. I. Bell's inventory

title: Grain Receipt
summary: Receipt for grain paid to Eusebios, ordinarius. by Timotheos on behalf of Anelios, kybernetes
provenance: Aphroditopolite
translation_text: The heir of Eusebios, ordinarius, has measured through Timotheos from the village Pensa under Anelios, captain of the ship of Ioannes Paener-, for the produce in the 14th indiction. [l. 5] Of the wheat by the cancellus measure twenty artabas . . . 20 only. without gratuity and additional payments. Signed by me, Eulogios.

title: Medical Text
summary: Prescription for eye salve: the papyrus, written on both sides in the same hand, contains two medical prescriptions complete with titles and directions for use; originally published as accounts for medical supplies; the recto contains a recipe for an eye salve of the type commonly called achariston
provenance: Unknown
translation_text: [recto] For children; burnt copper, 4 drachmas [i.e. 96 gr]; calamine, 6 or 8 drachmas; opium, 2 or 4 drachmas; myrrh, 2 1/3 drachmas; erica, 2 drachmas; acacia, 6 drachmas; gum, 6 drachmas; use water until it becomes sufficiently glutinous in thickness. [verso] Station at Fla-: my pepper, 2 drachmas [i.e. 48 g]; litharge, 2 drachmas; myrrh, 4 drachmas; fat, 2 drachmas; gum, 10 drachmas; use wine . . . gum and ingredients.

title: Receipt for Payment of a Debt
summary: Receipt issued by Neilos, son of Mysthos, to Aretion Nasonos for payment of a debt
translation_text: Neilos, son of Mysthes, to Aretion, son of Nason, through the son of Chairemon, greetings. I have received from you the appropriate part of what is owed to me . . .

title: Receipt for Loan
summary: Taseus, daughter of Pekysis, with her father acting as guardian, acknowledges the receipt of 300 drachmas of silver and interest.  Asklepiades acts as scribe.  Recorded by Alexander in charge of the record office of the middle toparchy
provenance: From Oxyrhynchus(?)
translation_text: [recto] I, Taseus, daughter of Pekysis, with guardian who is my father Pekysis son of Pekysis, acknowledge the receipt of 300 silver drachmas and the interest thereon, and I have no further claim as above. I, Dionysios son of Asklepiades, wrote on their behalf since they are [l. 5] illiterate. [hand 2] The 10th year of Tiberius Caesar Augustus. Mechir 25. Recorded by Alexander in charge of the record office of the middle toparchy. [verso] Taseus’ acknowledgement of receipt to Dion-.

title: Repayment of Loans
summary: Horos acknowledges to Ekysis the receipt of a sum which was owed to him by the latter.  The transaction appears to cover various debts previously incurred by Ekysis
provenance: From Theadelphia
translation_text: The seventh year of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrian Antoninus Augustus Pius, the 15th of Mesore, in the village of Theadelphia of the [l. 5] Themistes division of the Arsinoite nome. Horos, who has no legal father and whose mother is AÃ¼nchis, approximately forty-five years old, with a scar on the [l. 10] small finger of the left hand, acknowledges to Ekysis, son of Panesneus, approximately thirty-five years old, with a scar on the small finger of the left hand, to have received from him in full all [l. 15] what Ekysis ever owed to Horos in accordance with the written contract, and Horos, as well as those associated with him, does not and will not take any proceedings [l. 20] against Ekysis or those associated with him regarding [transactions during] the time from before and until the present day. I, Horos, who has no legal father and whose mother is AÃ¼nchis, have received [l. 25] from Ekysis all what he owed . . .

title: Repayment of a Loan [162 AD]
summary: Chairemon, son of Sarapion, his mother being Taplutas, acknowledges that he has received from Tryphon son of Herakleides, the sum of 600 silver drachmas plus interest which was owed to him.  The transaction took place before time, through the bank bank at the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus, the officials involved being Phanias, ex chief priest and Ptolemaios, both described as the overseers associated with Audasius Paulinus
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1914-15
translation_text: Chaeremon son of Sarapion son of . . ., his mother being Taplutas, of the city of Oxyrhynchus, to Tryphon son of Heraclides son of . . . on, his mother being Taapollonides, of the said city, greeting. I acknowledge that I have received from you, who are making payment of your own accord before the appointed term, through the bank at the Serapeum at Oxyrhynchus of Phanias, ex-chief priest, and Ptolemaeus, the overseers associated with Audasius Paulinus, the capital sum of six hundred silver drachmae with the interest thereon up to the present time at the rate of two drachmae per month, which sum was lent you by me in accordance with a note of hand through the said bank on the intercalary days of the second year of Antoninus and Verus, lords and Emperors, until . . .

title: Receipt [201/202 AD]
summary: Acknowledgment of receipt of 600 drachmas plus interest of a drachma per mina per month lent by Chr..ous, son(?) of Amois, his mother being Asklepia, to a man whose mother is Demetrous Both parties live in the hamlet of Dositheos
provenance: Given to Princeton University in 1957 by Edmund H. Kase in memory of Professor Allan Chester Johnson

title: Receipt
summary: Receipt for the repayment of a mixed loan of wheat and money lent to Aurelius Kollouthos by Aurelius Tithoes, son of Tithoes
provenance: From Oxyrhynchus
translation_text: In the consulship of Flavius Leontinus, the prefect of the sacred praetorium, and Salustius comes (count) . . . . Aurelius Tithoes, son of Tithoes, [l. 5] from the city of Oxyrhynchos, a . . . by trade, through me, his wife, Sophia, to Aurelius Kollouthos of the same city, [l. 10] greetings. I acknowledge to have received from you on account of things owed by you . . . artabas of wheat and . . . [l. 15] drachmas . . . the receipt is valid . . . and having been asked, I acknowledged . . . [consulship, month, date]

title: Receipt [578 AD]
summary: Receipt dated by the eras of Oxyrhyhnchus, for three sextarii of oil.  Received through Serenus, collector, by Makarios and Elias and Kamoul
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society

title: Receipt [ BC]
summary: Receipt for payment of rent, partly in olyra, partly in a money equivalent of wheat, by two farmers.  Land appears to be crown land.  The protocol contains a mention of the association of the gods Adelphi with Alexander in the Alexandrian cult.  Reference to the canephorus of Arsinoe is missing
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1907

title: Receipt for the Repayment of Rent (?)
summary: Receipt for payment of rent(?), with omologia formula.  Written by Papontos son of Nekpheros to Socrates son of Apollonios
provenance: From Philadelphia
translation_text: Papontos, son of Nekpheros . . .; to Sokrates, son of Apollonios . . . from Philadelphia. I acknowledge . . . in the fifth plot around Philadelphia . . . [l. 5] four arouras by the measure . . . for the payment of rent . . . each year . . . artabas of barley . . .

title: Receipts for Payment of Rent on Leased Land
summary: The text is divided into two columns, in each of which a separate receipt is recorded.  Both receipts are for payment of rent (ekphorion)made on a parcel of land situated near Tanis a small village a few miles south of Philadelphia.  The land is part of a chleros.  Aurelious Herakleides, ex-cosmete of Alexandria appears as lessor.  The land was leased by Aurelius Patron, Aurelius Asoeis and Aurelius Atammon.  The amount of rent paid in the first receipt is not stated, but in the second it is 656 1/2 artabas
provenance: From Tanis
translation_text: [col. I] Aurelius Herakleides, former kosmetes of the most illustrious city of Alexandria, through Amais, overseer of Tanis, to Aurelii Patron and Asoeis and Atammon, renters of the [l. 5] plot of land of Ptiap-, greetings. I have received from you the payment of rent for the arourai of my land which you farm on shares near the village of Tanis for the current second year, the rent having been paid in full, and having been formally questioned, I have acknowledged it. I, Aurelius Herakleides, agree as appropriate. [l. 10] The 2nd year of our Lords Valerianus and Gallienus, Augusti, the 6th added day of Mesore [sc. August 29, 255 AD]. [col. II] Aurelius Herakleides, former kosmetes of the most illustrious city of Alexandria, through Amais, [l. 15] overseer of Tanis, to Aurelii Patron and Asoeis and Atammon, renters of the plot of land of Ptiap-, greetings. I have received from you the payment of rent for the arourai of my land which you farm on shares [l. 20] near the village of Tanis for the current 3rd year . . . 656 artabas of wheat, half of the full . . . total 656 artabas of wheat. I have received through Amais 656 artabas of wheat, as has been agreed. [l. 25] The 3rd year of our Lords Valerianus and Gallienus, Augusti, [month, day].

title: Medical Text [middle I AD]
summary: Medical treatise on tumors

title: Receipt [V AD]
summary: Receipt for payments of wine made by Anoup and Johannes to Martyrios
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1914-15

title: Receipt [II/III AD]
summary: Receipt for payment received from Dionysios
provenance: Garrett Deposit 1924, no. XIVa 11 in H. I. Bell's inventory

title: Receipt for annona militum
summary: Receipt for 100 drachmas for the annona militum, paid by a high priest, Bitharion.  The tax may have been paid to a female tax collector
provenance: From Oxyrhynchus
translation_text: The 3rd year of our lords Valerianus and Gallienus Augusti, the month of Choiak. Aurelius Bitharion, also called Eudaimon, high-priest, paid to the apaitetai (=collectors) of the western toparchy [l. 5] for the annona of 12,000 soldiers one hundred drachmas, totals 100 drachmas. At the estate of Ptolemais, through me, [l. 10] Thonis overseer and member of the town senate, it has been signed.

title: Receipt [244/43 BC]
summary: Receipt for the brewing tax in the amount of eighteen drachmas for the month of Pachon paid by Petosiris, agent of Taembes.  At the end are the signature of Dorion and a line of demotic
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1907

title: Receipt [178 AD]
summary: Receipt for dike tax for Soknopaiou Nesos in the amount of 7 dr. 4 ob. 2 chalk
provenance: Given to Princeton University in 1957 by Edmund H. Kase in memory of Professor Allan Chester Johnson

title: Receipts for demosia
summary: Receipt for payment of 2 7/24 carats for tax, the demosia. Could refer to the land tax in particular or public taxes in general.  Received by Kollouthos also called Stephanos
provenance: Provenance Unknown
translation_text: Holding of Hermaion, from the heirs. Kollouthos, also called Stephanos, has received from you in full the payment for demosia (a public tax) of the sixth indiction for the third part, two and one sixth and one eighth of keratia in gold, totals 2 1/6 1/8 keratia, and for your guarantee I have made the receipt, as appropriate. [l. 5] Kollouthos, also called Stephanos. [fragment of second receipt] . . . from Ammonios, riparius (= a police official); I have received in full from you; . . . sixteen keratia, total 16 ker.; . . . and for your guarantee I have made the receipt; [l. 10] . . . as appropriate. Kollouthos also called Stephanos.

title: Receipt for embole
summary: Receipt for 30 artabas of grain collected for land tax
provenance: From the Hermopolite nome(?)
translation_text: For the embole of the eighth indiction from the village of Moirai, through Ioannes of the holding of Ieremias, thirty artabas of wheat by the sallarian (or solenikos?) measure, totals 30 artabas of wheat by the sallarian (or solenikos?) measure. Written on Epeiph 12, 8th indiction, by Viktor, who agrees to it.For the embole of the eighth indiction from the village of Moirai, through Ioannes of the holding of Ieremias, thirty artabas of wheat by the sallarian (or solenikos?) measure, totals 30 artabas of wheat by the sallarian (or solenikos?) measure. Written on Epeiph 12, 8th indiction, by Viktor, who agrees to it.

title: Receipts for merismos
summary: Receipt for tax, the merismos.  This and P.Princ. 91 were written by the same hand and issued to the same person, Makarios son of Kerekos(?), in different months of the same year presumably as installments for the tax
provenance: From Kerkesoucha Orous
translation_text: 16 Hathyr, for the merismos of the 3rd indiction in Kerkesoucha Orrous, Makarios, son of Kerekos, twenty two and a quarter keratia, totals only 22 1/4 at this time. Through me, secretary Georgios, with God. [l. 5] 23 Choiak, the same person, fourteen keratia, totals only 14 ker. at this time. Through me, secretary Georgios, with God.

title: Receipt [78 AD]
summary: Receipt for nine(?) obols of copper paid for dike work
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1901

title: Lyric Poetry [late III AD]
summary: Sailor's song, a prayer to the Rhodian winds for a calm voyage; the title, 'To the Rhodian', winds is written in the margin
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1922

title: Receipt [80/81 AD]
summary: Receipt for nine(?) obols of copper paid an unidentified tax
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1901

title: Receipt [135-6 AD]
summary: Receipt for for various taxes, including dike work
provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society, 1901

title: Receipt for Poll-Tax (andrismos)
summary: Receipt for one sixth of a solidus paid for poll tax
provenance: From the Hermopolite nome(?)
translation_text: 10 Thoth, I received through Souiros for the andrismos (= a poll-tax) of the 10th indiction 1/6 arithmion (sc. solidus) through Menas, son of Phoibammon, apaitetas, who agrees.

title: Receipt for Poll-Tax (diagraphe)
summary: Receipt for 3 1/2 carats paid for poll tax paid by Dioskoros on behalf of Phoibammon
provenance: Aphrodite kome (Antaeopolite nome)
translation_text: Apollos, son of Dioskoros, gave on behalf of Phoibammon for the poll-tax of the 2nd indiction three and a half keratia in gold, total 3 1/2 ker., and I am prepared to bring to you the receipt of the polis-steward and to retrieve mine. I, Petros the cashier, agree to this.

title: Receipts for Poll-Tax (laographia)
summary: Three receipts for payment of twenty drachmas each from three brothers, Chairemon, Herakles(?)  and Diodoros for poll tax.  All have the same father, Chairemon; the mother of Herakles and Diodoros is Apia, Chairemon's mother being Diodora
provenance: From Arsionoe
translation_text: The 4th year of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, the 16th of Pachon, calculated for Pharmouthi, Chairemon, son of Chairemon, also called Apollonios, whose mother is Diodora, paid on account of the poll-tax of the fourth year at the Syrian quarter twenty drachmas, totals 20; the surtax 10 obols in bronze. The 4th year of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, the 16th of Pachon, calculated for Pharmouthi, Herakles, son of Chairemon, also called Chairemon, whose mother is Apia, paid on account of the poll-tax of the fourth year at the Syrian quarter twenty drachmas, totals 20; the surtax 10 obols in bronze. The 4th year of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, the 16th of Pachon, calculated for Pharmouthi, Didodoros son of Chairemon, also [called Chairemon, whose mother is Ap]ia, paid on account of the poll-tax of the fourth year at the Syrian quarter twenty drachmas, totals 20; the surtax 10 obols in bronze.

title: Receipt for Poll-Tax (laographia)
summary: Receipt for 20 drachmas, 10 obols received as poll tax from a resident of the Macedonian quarter of Arsinoe, Polios Horos, son of Pathermouthios, his mother being Taeus
provenance: From Arsinoe
translation_text: The 14th year of Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, the lord, Armeniacus Medicus Parthicus Germanicus Maximus, the 12th of Mesore, on calculation for the month Epeiph, Polion s. of [l. 5] Heron, also called Pathermouthios, whose mother is Taeus, paid as a poll-tax for the fourteenth year in the Macedonian quarters, twenty drachmas, totals 20 drachmas, the surtax is 10 obols in bronze.

title: Receipt for Poll-Tax
summary: Receipt for 44 drachmas paid in several installments for the poll tax
translation_text: The 5th year . . . also called Arios . . . paid on account of the poll-tax for the same 5th year in the village of Philadelphia four drachmas, totals [l. 5] 4 drachmas; in Choiak, 4 drachmas, in Tybi, 8 drachmas; in Pharmouthi, 8 drachmas; in Pachon, 4 drachmas; in Pauni, 8 drachmas; likewise in Mesore, 8 drachmas.

title: Receipt [132 AD]

title: Receipt [II AD]

title: Receipt for Poll-Tax (syntaximon)
summary: Receipt for 12 drachmas, 1 1/2 obols, 2 chalci received in partial payment of the poll tax, together with smaller sums paid on various taxes
provenance: From the Arsinoite
translation_text: The fifth year of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, the 5th of Choiak, Heron s. of Phaseis s. of Chairemon whose mother is Thaisas paid through Didymos and the assistants of the tax collectors on account of the poll-tax for the same year twelve drachmas and a half-obol(?) in silver and 2 chalki, totals [l. 5] 12 dr. 1/2 obol 2 chalki; on account of the pig-tax, 1 dr. 1 obol, on account of the tax concerning manning the watch-tower and guarding, 1 dr. 3 obols, on account of the prison-tax and the river-guard tax, 2 1/2 obols.

title: Oracle Question [II AD]

provenance: Given to Princeton University by the Egypt Exploration Society

title: Receipt for telos moschou thuomenou

provenance: Arsinoite nome
translation_text: The 14th year of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius, Augusti, and Publius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus, the 4th of Pachon. Twenty drachmas were paid to [l. 5] Aurelius Apion, nomarch of the Arsinoite nome, through Sarapion, administrator, on account of the tax for tomorrow's sacrifice of a calf in the village of Theadelphia (?) by Onesimos s. of Amerimnos . . .

title: Receipt for the Trades' Tax
summary: Receipt for 60 drachmas, 2 obols, 2 chalci for payment of trades' tax. The trade for which the tax was paid is not specified.  An additional payment of 3 drachmas, 3 obols is also recorded, evidently representing a subsequent installment of the same tax
provenance: From Philadelphia
translation_text: The tenth year of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus Emperor, month of Pharmouthi. From Isidoros and Maron, son of Maron the younger, craftsmen, for the syntaximon of the same [l. 5] tenth year at Philadelphial sixty drachmas in silver, 2 obols and 2 chalki has been paid, totals 60 dr. 2 ob. 2 ch; also three drachmas 3 obols, totals 3 dr. 3 ob.

title: Receipt

provenance: From Oxyrhynchus
translation_text: Aurelius Ptolemion exegete and Aurelius Herakleianos, also called Morion, chief priest, both senators of the distinguished and most distinguished city of Oxyrhynchos, managers [l. 5] of the public bank, greet Aurelia Sintotoe, also called Aleitarion. You have paid into the public bank of the nome for the reckoning of the month Choiak on behalf of . . . [l. 10] in the hamlet of Taotri . . . of the current 19th year and the 18th year the sum of three thousand silver drachmas, totals 3,000. The 19th and the 18th year [l. 15] of our Caesars Diocletian and Maximian, Augusti, and the 11th year of Constantine and Maximian, the most noble Caesars. Pauni 1(.).

title: Register of Taxes
summary: Register of payments for the syntaximon; entries contain a name, date and amount paid, in each case installments of 4 drachmas, or multiples of 4; a space of two lines was left under each name for the record of his payments, which were entered by a second clerk; the names are not arranged alphabetically and the record is probably that of a street or quarter of Philadelphia; there are twelve names in all; check marks are beside the five names of those who have paid in full

title: Register of Taxes
summary: Tax register for payments of the syntaximon for the year 29-30 AD; names are arranged alphabetically with name, patronymic and occupation or some other descriptive epithet; on average, twelve names were entered in each column; the roll has a series of checks and marginal notations; two sloping parallel indicate that payment has been made in full; before several names a single dot appears, perhaps indicating that the entry had been checked against that in a day book; marginal notations indicate persons who have fled and those who are exempt from taxation
provenance: From Philadelphia

title: Letter
summary: Letter of condolence on the loss of a son, containing the phrase "the son of god"; the writer expresses his regret that he cannot come to the bereaved parent
translation_text: To my lord brother Kerdon, Alexander sends greetings. I would have wished to rush to you straight from here and [l. 5] pay due honor to the benevolence of your blessed son, but my not being able to travel and meet you has got into the way, since it is difficult for us to [l. 10] go up to the city. It is necessary to bear the human lot. Therefore, put away the grief of human fault and think that nobody among men is [l. 15] immortal, but only God, and remember the promise of the blessed Paul, as . . . [margin] I pray that you be well for many years. [verso] To my lord brother Kerdon.

title: Registration of Livestock
summary: Registration of live stock, sheep and goats, for the current year; addressed to the strategos of the Oxyrhynchite nome, whose name may be Chaereas; presented to the toparch Sarapion who functions in a similar capacity in P.Oxy, 351
provenance: From Oxyrhynchus
translation_text: Sheep 109, goats 3. To Chaereas, the strategos from Taarchorsia. [l. 5] . . . I am registering for the current 7th year of Tiberius Caesar Augustus the 109 sheep and [l. 10] 3 goats that belong to me. Total: 109 goats, 3 sheep, and the lambs and kids that are their offspring, which will be herded by Pela towards the western [l. 15] toparchy and throughout the entire pasture by the shepherd Petseiris, son of Petseiris, who is registered as residing by Sesphtha of the lower toparchy, and on these flocks [l. 20] I will also pay the tax that is due. ____ Farewell. I, toparch Sarapion, have registered one hundred [l. 25] nine sheep, three goats, totals 109 sheep, 3 goats. The 7th year of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, Tybi the 30th.

title: Registration of Livestock
summary: Registration of live stock, covering a flock of 15 sheep, 10 lambs and 3 goats for the year 218/219;  it would appear that an identical return had been made in the previous year; the flock subsequently suffered a loss of 10 sheep, which was balanced by the birth of 10 lambs;  the lambs of the previous year were numbered among the sheep of the current year
provenance: From Oxyrhynchus
translation_text: . . . I have registered in the passed first year at the same village full-grown sheep 15, lambs 10, goats 3; the total from the previous year to the current [l. 5] year: full-grown sheep 25, goats 3, of which the loss in accordance with register and count was for the current year full-grown sheep 10. This leaves full-grown sheep 15, goats 3 [[which I have registered]], [l. 10] and following the birth in the first year of 10 lambs, the total is [[25]], which totals for the second year at the same village, 25 sheep. Of these in the same first year in the same village there was a loss of 10 new born lambs. The sheep will remain at the same village and in the grazing area of the shepherd Ammonios. And I swear by the fate of [l. 15] Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus not to have lied. The 2nd year of Emperor Caesar [l. 20] Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, I swear by his fate not to have lied. The 2nd year of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius [l. 25] Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, 5th of Mecheir. Aurelius Ammonius Ptolatos through me . . . I have given and sworn the oath as appropriate.

title: Registration of a Mortgage [ca. 98-99  AD]
summary: Registration of a mortgage addressed to an agoranomos by Caecilius Clemens, an official whose status is not specified; the mortgage is made out to Thonis, chief bearer in the temple of Thoeris and Isis and Sarapis and Osiris, on the security of the third part of a house situated in the Gymnasium square quarter and mortgaged to him by his brother Thomphuas; to this notification is added a banker's certificate that the tax on sales and mortgages had been paid
provenance: From Oxyrhynchus
translation_text: Caecilius Clemens to the agoranomus, greetings. Register a contract of loan from Thonis, son of Harpaesis, son of Petserothonis, his mother being Petosiris, daughter of Harpaesis, of the city of Oxyrhynchus, chief bearer in the temple of Thoeris and Isis and Sarapis and Osiris and the associated most mighty gods, on the security of the third part of a house, in which there is a hall, with the court and entrances and exits and appurtenances, situated in the Gymnasium square quarter by the temple of Osiris and the treasury, which was mortgaged to him by his full brother Thomphuas in return for an accommodation in accordance with a note of hand and a payment through a bank of 400 drachmae...,and...

title: Report of Legal Proceedings
summary: Portion of an official record of a trial held in the court of the chrematistai, sitting in Crocodilopolis; details of the case remains obscure but it seems that someone connected with the case had been instructed on the thirteenth of Mecheir, in the twenty-third year of an unnamed ruler, to appear in court on the same day to attend the hearing of the case; his appearance in court was prevented, however, by the fact that on the same day he was occupied in taking a letter from the praktor in Crocodilopolis to the epistates in the village of Philadelphia
provenance: From Crocodilopolis
translation_text: son of Isidoros also called . . . of the village of Alexandreus, but not yet enrolled in the Hephaistiea deme, dwelling in Philadelphia of the Herakleides meris of the same village . . . of the court of [l. 5] chrematistai, of whom Theogenes was the introducer of the case, of the 23rd year, the 11th of Mecheir. Over 4 arouras of the vineyard, from which annually the produce [at the amount of 33 keramia of the new wine] is taken. Summoned through Pelops the attendant, the 23rd year, the 13th of Mecheir. (2nd hand) How is it possible, with the summons [l. 10] taking place on the 13th, on the day on which I took the letter from the praktor to the epistate of Philadelphia, to make an appeal for renewal of the action immediately on that same day in Krokodilopolis, [l. 15] while I was that far from the polis on that day. Even if somebody tried to come to the polis, he would not catch the chrematistai still sitting, which would be most frustrating. I took the letter from the epistate to [l. 20] the praktor on the 14th, and on the 15th I appealed to the chrematistai through Demetrios the attendant.

title: Memorandum and Speech of an Advocate
summary: Rhetorical presentation of a case at law, which contains a summary of the speech in the left margin; the case involves a parcel of land owned by Theon and used for planting grain and  for vineyard; the plaintiff, Dioskoros, represented by his son Makrobios, charges tax evasion and his claim centers on the vineyard which he claims is unregistered for tax purposes; the defendants claim that the land is registered but offer no evidence to this effect
translation_text: [col. I] Registering land in the allotment of Nikanor, father Theon also brought other land under cultivation and farmed it, and he planted this land (i.e. the land in dispute) in vines. But in the time of Sarapion, [l. 5] the rationalis, it happened that this was measured up and was registered in a supplementary report . . . tax assessments . . . But Dioskoros through his son [l. 10] introduced impious libel . . . and even calumny. Yet he agrees that the property was held and farmed by us; but the vineyard he says is unregistered and [l. 15] he thinks he has the right to declare this land. Having openly practiced deception, he has also made himself liable to the charge and it is clear . . . it is unjust to tamper with other people's [l. 20] property . . . [col. II] Narratio: You speak on behalf of Dionysios, ex-protector, and Ammonios, beneficiarius. The opponent (is) Dioskoros or his son Makrobios. Those who tamper with other people's property and who attempt an accusation, [l. 4] both the laws and your own hatred of evil despise. Precisely this Dioskoros will be shown to have done and he is worthy of punishment, as will be clear from the things which are going to be said. When Theon, father of my [l. 8] client and brother of Dionysios, was still living, he registered property in the allotment of Nikanor during the reign of the divine Diocletian and he also purchased other land; and because he was industrious, he went to considerable expense and he planted a part of his land in vines, while the other land [l. 12] he also brought under cultivation in grain and he continued to farm it and to hold possession of it. Much time has passed from then until the present, and the children came into possession after the death of their father. But in those days when Sarapion was holding [l. 16] the office of rationalis and new vineyard was being measured up and father Theon was still alive, it happened that also that land which had been cultivated as vineyard was subjected to measuring and became tax-bearing and [l. 20] was registered in a supplementary report for my clients, and in point of fact they pay the military annonas to the most sacred fiscus for it. But Dioskoros, or his son in his father's name as we just now learned, [l. 24] boldly brings before your grace an utterly libelous charge. Now on the one hand, even he agrees that from the beginning the land was both farmed by us and [col. III] planted in vines, but he lies when he says that it pays no tax and [l. 28] contributes nothing whatever to the most sacred fiscus, since as we said, even the part planted by us in vines was subsequently measured up by the censitor who was sent out and it became taxpaying. But he demanded that the things [l. 32] granted by them and improved be surrendered to him, which no law ever permits. Since it is not at all fitting to tamper with other people's properties, whether these are based on claims more or less founded, [l. 36] but since the laws confirm those who have worked hard, and since he wanted to introduce a libelous charge also he openly acknowledged that these were held and farmed by us, we request on the one hand that nothing be changed and that the indictment [l. 40] which he deceitfully invented accomplish nothing, and on the other hand he be produced (in court) through your office as one who stands liable to the charge by reason of the claims that they submitted. If he should say that "You agree that the land is unregistered," [l. 44] we shall say that it is he who says that the vineyard is unregistered. For we declare that since new vineyard land was measured at that time, this land was both submitted to survey long ago and pays the taxes required by the fiscus. Even if [l. 48] the censitor who may be sent out should find, when he has done his measuring, that a bit more has been improved by us, we do not then beg off from both registering and paying in full the sacred taxes in the same way as the province does. Meanwhile then, since he is introducing a malicious practice [col. IV] [l. 52] and so to speak to outright calumny which the laws and our lords hate, [l. 56] we beg you to order that his attempt be without result for him and that the countercharges brought by him accomplish nothing.

title: Roman Will
summary: Will drawn up in the Roman form by Aurelia Serenilla, also called Demetria, who names her mother as heir;  her sons are declared to be disinherited; bequests of land are made to her husband who also acts as kyrios, as well as to the man who acts as her kourator
provenance: From Hermopolis
translation_text: Aurelia Serenilla, also called Demetria, daughter of Philippianos, also called Kopreas, a former councilor of the great, ancient, illustrious and most illustrious city of Hermopolis, with her guardian Aurelius Herminos, also called Achilles, son of Eudaimon, eutheniarch of the same city of Hermopolis and with a curator Aurelius Valerius Longus, veteran, of the same city of Hermopolis, had composed a will and dictated it to be written. Aurelia Asklaterion, also called Koprilla, my mother, shall be my heiress, all other persons being disinherited. She shall proceed to my inheritance in 100 designated days, [l. 5] counting from when she learned and became able to testify herself that she is my heiress. To my husband Aurelius Achilles, also called Herminos, kosmetes, I leave the plot of land by the assigned impost of the native troops by Ibyon Peteaphthi from the estate of Naube, of which I hold in one quarter . . . arouras. . . five arouras and around the same village from the commonality of the marriage of Aline of which I hold three arouras . . . . . to my curator . . .

title: Letter

provenance: From Oxyrhynchus
translation_text: To the most honorable Phoibammon, assistant of the protocometae of the village Koba from Flavianus, advocate. Be disposed to entertain for one evening Ioannes, also called Pet[ ], advocate, who is going with god up to Oxyrhynchos. Also furnish some hay to his six pack animals on that one evening. [verso] Give it to the most honorable Phoibammon, assistant of the protocometae of the village Koba, from Flavianus, advocate.
